name: Black Olive, Sun-Dried Tomato, and Celery

ingredients.item: celery stalks / 2 handfuls of pitted black olives / a handful of sun-dried tomatoes (from a jar, in oiO / 2 tablespoons
to rest fo r a n hour before se rving

method: mix well • You can either serve the oil like this or you can strain it, really squeezing the flavors
to give you a lovely, smooth oil
BASIL AND LEMON OIL
8 tablespoons extra virgin olive oil / juice of I lemon / a few sprigs offresh basil / sea salt and freshly ground black
To make this you can use a pestle and mortar, a food processor, or a blender. Add t he extra virgin olive
juice, and basil to the blender and add a good pinch of salt and pepper . Whiz together until
you have a smooth oil • You can serve it like this or pass it through a strainer to give you a much
smoother oil • It can be kept in the fridge for a couple of days • Give it a quick shake before serving
CHOPPED HERB OIL
a small handful of mixed fresh soft green herbs (use one type or a mixture of basil, parsley, and mint) /
extra virgin olive oil / red wine vinegar / sea salt and freshly ground black pepper
Finely chop the herbs. Put them into a bowl with the extra virgin olive oil, a swig of vinegar, and a good
of salt and pepper. Mix well before serving
CHILE AND MINT OIL
I fresh red chile / a few sprigs of fresh mint / 8 tablespoons extra virgin olive oil / juice of ~ a lemon / sea salt
and freshly ground black pepper
Halve the red chile and seed it if you don't want your oil to be too spicy • Finely chop t he chile. Pick
mint leaves and finely chop. Put the chile and mint into a bowl with the extr a virgin olive oil, lemon
juice, and a good pinch of salt and pepper. Mix well before serving
276 QUICK-COOKING MEAT AND FISH
So many people are scared to cook fish. It's such a shame there's so much fuss
about it , but I'm afraid fish is just another casualty of us becoming detached
from our food in modern-day life. I couldn't count the number of people I've
come across who say, "No, don't like fish," and I ask, "But what have you tried?"
and t hey say, "Salmon," and it transpires that they've only had a bit of dry salmon
that's had the life cooked out of it for fifty minutes. "Hate fish, it stinks," and so
it goes on .... But it's such an incredible source of protein, and there are so many
flavors and textures that you can get from cooking it in different ways
whet her it's barbecued, broiled, roasted, or steamed, there's a whole world of
fish o ut there to be enjoyed.
There's some great fish coming through the frozen markets now, so don't feel
that you'vl"> always got to buy fresh. However, when you do buy fresh, use your
sense of sme I -your instincts are nor mally always right, so if it smells, don't buy
If it looks rubbish, don't buy it. Salmon farming seems to be getting better these
days, which is a good thing as wild stocks are diminishing. And frozen fish is
starting to be represented by some great products, not just junk and trimmings.
If there was a Holy G rail of fish shopping, it would be this: get to know your local
fishmonger. Once you have a good relationship with him, the rest will fall into place.
But groce ry stores seem to be doing a better and better job, certainly with
prepacked and frozen fish.
You may never have thought of buying fish online, and
at the number of fishermen and producers who sell their produce
directly like this. So there are lots of options, and not so many excuses anymore.
To cut a long story short, we all need to eat more fish. All I'll say is that the
Japanese have the longest life expectancy in the world, and they eat mainly fish.
SALMON FISH CAKES
Not only do homemade fish cakes taste miles better than store-bought ones, but if you make your own you
what goes into them: the cheap factory-made ones can often be fille d with rubbish.This recipe
is so tasty that it's a really good idea to double or triple the quantities and freeze batches for an ot her day
- just make sure you defrost them thoroughly before us ing, then follow the cooking instructions below.
makes 8 fish cakes
sea salt and freshly ground black pepper
I ~ pounds potatoes
salmon fillets, skin on, scaled and bones
To prepare your fish cakes
a small bunch of fresh Italian parsley
I tablespoon all-purpose flour, plus extra for dusting
I large egg, preferably free-range or organic
Bring a large pan of salted water to a boil. Peel your potatoes and chop them int o eve n-sized chunks
• Rub the salmon fillets all over with olive oil and a pinch of salt and pepper. Add the potatoes to the
pan and bring back to a boil. Put the fish into a colander, covered with aluminum foil, and place it over
the pan of potatoes. Turn the heat down and cook for 10 to 12 minutes, until the potatoes and fish are
both cooked • Remove the fish from the colander and put on one side • Drain the po tatoes in the
colander, then return them to the pan and let them steam dry. Pick the parsley leaves and finely chop
them, discarding the stalks. Mash the potatoes, spreading the potato around the sides of the pan to help
it cool down quickly. Remove any skin from the fish • When the potato is completely cool, put it into
bowl and flake the fish into it with I tablespoon of flour. Add the egg and chopped parsley w ith a really
good pinch of salt and pepper. Finely grate over the lemon zest, then mash and mix it all up well
To make your fish cakes
Dust your work surface with flour. Divide your fish cake mixture into 8 • Lightly shape and pat into
about 0/4 inch thick, dusting them with flour as you go • Get yourself a plate or tray, dust it with
flour, and place your fish cakes on top. If you're going to freeze them at this point, wrap them in plastiC
wrap and put them into the freezer. Otherwise simply pop them into the refrigerator for an hour before
will allow them to firm up slightly
To cook and serve your fish cakes
Put a large frying pan on a medium heat and add a couple of lugs of olive oil. When the oi l is nice and
hot, add your fish cakes and cook for about 3 to 4 minutes on each side or until crisp and golde n - you
to cook them in two batches • Serve straightaway, with lemon halves for squeezing over, and
some peas, broccoli, greens, or salad. These fish cakes also go really well w ith the tomato, olive, basil, and
chile sauce on page 269
280 CLASSIC FISH
SMOKED MACKEREL pATE
This is such a lovely thing to eat. There's loads of goodness in the skin of the mackerel, so try to leave it
on when you make this - you won't really notice it once the fish is all chopped up. However, if you don't
fancy eating the skin you can remove it.
